name: Guatemala
tagline: From the heartland of Classic Maya civilisation through Spanish conquest and a long, painful path to peace.
summary: Guatemala is the heartland of Maya civilisation, home to the great Classic city of Tikal and to the K'iche' Maya whose sacred Popol Vuh survives as a masterwork of Mesoamerican literature. The Spanish conquest under Pedro de Alvarado in 1524 opened three centuries of colonial rule from Santiago de los Caballeros, today's Antigua Guatemala, until independence from Spain on 15 September 1821. A coffee economy and a succession of Liberal dictatorships gave way to the democratic October Revolution of 1944, ended by a CIA-backed coup in 1954; there followed a thirty-six-year civil war in which, by the United Nations-backed truth commission's estimate, some 200,000 people were killed or disappeared, the great majority of them Maya. Peace accords in 1996 closed the conflict, and Guatemala remains a young democracy contending with the legacy of war, inequality, and corruption.

founding.label: Independence from Spain
founding.detail: On 15 September 1821 a council of notables in Guatemala City signed the Act of Independence of Central America, severing the Captaincy General of Guatemala from Spanish rule after nearly three centuries. The former kingdom was briefly annexed to the Mexican empire of Agustin de Iturbide before joining the United Provinces of Central America, which in 1824 adopted the constitution of the Federal Republic of Central America, a federation of Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica. The federation dissolved amid civil war by 1840, leaving Guatemala a sovereign republic.

quickFacts.label: Maya heartland
quickFacts.value: Tikal flourished c. 600-800 CE; collapse by c. 900

quickFacts.label: Colonial capital
quickFacts.value: Santiago de los Caballeros (Antigua), founded 16th c.

eras.title: The Maya Heartland
eras.standfirst: Across the lowland forests of the Peten, the Maya built one of the great civilisations of the ancient world, whose largest city, Tikal, rose to dominance before the Classic collapse.
body: Settlement at Tikal, deep in the rainforest of what is now the northern department of Peten, began as a small village in the Middle Formative period, around 900 to 300 BCE, and grew into a major ceremonial centre in the centuries that followed. Like other lowland Maya centres, it developed monumental architecture, a hieroglyphic script, sophisticated calendars, and a mathematics that employed the concept of zero. Towering temple-pyramids, carved stelae recording the deeds of kings, and vast plazas marked it as a seat of sacred and dynastic power.

Between roughly 600 and 800 CE Tikal reached its architectural and artistic peak, a city-state of tens of thousands locked in shifting alliances and rivalries with other great centres such as Calakmul. The Maya of this Classic period had no single empire but a constellation of competing kingdoms, each ruled by a divine king whose legitimacy rested on warfare, ritual, and descent. The achievements of their astronomers, scribes, and architects make the Classic Maya the most sophisticated literate culture of the pre-Columbian Americas.

From about 800 CE a decline set in across the southern lowlands. At Tikal monument-building ceased, the population fell, and the urban core was largely abandoned by around 900. Scholars associate this Classic Maya collapse with a combination of pressures, among them prolonged drought, overpopulation, deforestation, and chronic warfare. The forest reclaimed the temples, but the Maya themselves did not vanish: their descendants endured, and Tikal National Park was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1979.

events.title: Tikal settled in the Peten
events.summary: A village forms at Tikal in the Middle Formative period and grows into a ceremonial centre of the lowland Maya.

events.title: Tikal at its height
events.summary: Tikal reaches its architectural and artistic peak as one of the dominant city-states of the Classic Maya world.

events.title: Classic Maya collapse
events.summary: Tikal and the southern lowland centres are depopulated and abandoned as the Classic Maya order disintegrates.

pullquote.text: Tikal contains the remains of one of the greatest cities of the ancient Maya, abandoned at the close of the 10th century.
pullquote.attribution: On Tikal National Park, UNESCO World Heritage Centre

eras.title: Postclassic Kingdoms and Spanish Conquest
eras.standfirst: In the highlands the K'iche' and other Maya peoples built new kingdoms and preserved the Popol Vuh, until Pedro de Alvarado's invasion of 1524 brought them under Spanish rule.
body: After the lowland collapse, the centre of Maya life shifted to the western highlands, where Postclassic peoples founded new states. The most powerful was the kingdom of the K'iche', ruled from the fortified capital of Q'umarkaj, also called Utatlan, near present-day Santa Cruz del Quiche. It was among the K'iche' that the Popol Vuh took shape, the sacred book of creation that recounts the making of the world and the deeds of the Hero Twins, Hunahpu and Xbalanque; the surviving text was written in K'iche' in the mid-sixteenth century and later copied by the friar Francisco Ximenez.

In early 1524 Pedro de Alvarado, a lieutenant of Hernan Cortes, marched south from newly conquered Mexico with a force of Spaniards and thousands of indigenous allies. He defeated the K'iche' in battle, by tradition killing their war-leader Tecun Uman, and seized and burned Q'umarkaj. Playing rival Maya peoples against one another, allying with the Kaqchikel before turning on them as well, Alvarado subdued much of the highlands within a few years, though resistance in remote regions persisted for decades.

Alvarado founded the first Spanish capital under the name Santiago de los Caballeros de Guatemala, governing as the territory's first captain general. The early capital was destroyed by a flood and mudslide from the Agua volcano in 1541, and in 1543 the colonial seat was re-established at the site now called Antigua Guatemala. There the Captaincy General of Guatemala, embracing most of Central America, would be ruled for more than two centuries.

events.title: Conquest under Pedro de Alvarado
events.summary: Alvarado invades from Mexico, defeats the K'iche', and burns their capital Q'umarkaj, beginning Spanish rule in Guatemala.

events.title: Santiago de los Caballeros re-founded
events.summary: After a flood destroys the earlier capital, the colonial seat of the Captaincy General is established at present-day Antigua Guatemala.

events.title: The Popol Vuh set down
events.summary: The K'iche' creation epic, the Popol Vuh, is written in alphabetic K'iche', preserving Maya mythology and dynastic history.

figures.name: Pedro de Alvarado
figures.blurb: Lieutenant of Cortes who led the conquest of Guatemala in 1524, defeating the K'iche' and Kaqchikel and founding the first Spanish capital as captain general.

pullquote.text: The Popol Vuh is the single most important source of knowledge of ancient Maya mythology and culture.
pullquote.attribution: On the K'iche' book of creation

eras.title: Colony, Independence, and Federation
eras.standfirst: For nearly three centuries Guatemala was the seat of Spanish power in Central America, until independence in 1821 led through a short-lived federation to a conservative republic.
body: From Antigua the Captaincy General of Guatemala governed a realm stretching from Chiapas to Costa Rica. The city became one of the great colonial centres of the Americas, rich in baroque churches, convents, and a university, its society ordered by a hierarchy of Spaniards, criollos, mestizos, and a Maya majority bound to tribute and forced labour. Repeated earthquakes culminated in the catastrophic Santa Marta tremors of 1773, which ruined Antigua and prompted the move of the capital to the present Guatemala City; the abandoned colonial city was inscribed by UNESCO in 1979.

On 15 September 1821 the colonial elite of Guatemala City signed the Act of Independence of Central America, ending Spanish rule without a war of liberation. After a brief and unpopular annexation to Iturbide's Mexican empire, the region declared itself the United Provinces of Central America and in 1824 adopted the constitution of the Federal Republic of Central America. Guatemala was its largest and most populous member and the seat of its government.

The federation was riven from the start by conflict between Liberals, who favoured anticlerical reform and free trade, and Conservatives, who defended the Church and colonial order. Civil war and the revolt led by the peasant caudillo Rafael Carrera tore the union apart; it dissolved by 1840, and Guatemala formally proclaimed itself a sovereign republic in 1847. Under Carrera and his Conservative successors the country turned inward, restoring Church privileges and preserving much of the old social structure until a Liberal revolution in 1871.

events.title: Santa Marta earthquakes ruin Antigua
events.summary: Powerful earthquakes devastate the colonial capital, leading to the transfer of government to present-day Guatemala City.

events.title: Independence from Spain
events.summary: Guatemala City signs the Act of Independence of Central America, ending nearly three centuries of Spanish rule.

events.title: Federal Republic of Central America
events.summary: The former captaincy general forms a federation of five states, with Guatemala City as its capital.

events.title: Republic of Guatemala proclaimed
events.summary: With the federation dissolved, Guatemala declares itself a sovereign republic under Conservative rule.

pullquote.text: Antigua, the capital of the Captaincy-General of Guatemala, was largely destroyed by an earthquake in 1773.

eras.title: Coffee and the Liberal Dictatorships
eras.standfirst: The Liberal revolution of 1871 remade Guatemala around coffee exports, foreign capital, and a succession of strongmen, deepening the inequalities that would later erupt in revolution.
body: The Liberal revolution of 1871, led by Miguel Garcia Granados and Justo Rufino Barrios, overthrew the Conservative order and set Guatemala on a course of export-led modernisation. Barrios, who dominated as president until his death in 1885, built railways and ports, curbed the power of the Church, and made coffee the backbone of the economy. To supply the new plantations he expropriated communal and Church lands and revived forced-labour drafts that fell most heavily on the Maya, binding the indigenous majority to the estates of a small planter elite.

Coffee tied Guatemala to the world market and to foreign investors, prominent among them German planters and, from the early twentieth century, the United States. The Boston-based United Fruit Company acquired vast landholdings, the railways, and the principal port, becoming the country's largest landowner and employer, an economic power so pervasive it was known as el pulpo, the octopus. Its influence over successive governments would make it central to the crises of the mid-century.

Political life remained authoritarian. After the long rule of Manuel Estrada Cabrera, the Great Depression brought to power General Jorge Ubico in 1931. Ubico governed for thirteen years through repression of unions and political opponents, while cultivating close ties to the United States and United Fruit. His regime preserved the coffee oligarchy and the labour system that underpinned it, until mounting urban and middle-class discontent forced his resignation in 1944.

events.title: Liberal revolution
events.summary: Justo Rufino Barrios and the Liberals seize power, launching coffee-based modernisation and the expropriation of communal and Maya lands.

events.title: The coffee economy
events.summary: Coffee becomes Guatemala's dominant export, financing railways and ports while binding the Maya to plantation labour.

events.title: Rise of the United Fruit Company
events.summary: United Fruit acquires extensive lands, railways, and the main port, becoming Guatemala's largest landowner and a dominant economic force.

events.title: Dictatorship of Jorge Ubico
events.summary: General Ubico rules for thirteen years, repressing labour and opposition while sustaining the coffee oligarchy and ties to United Fruit.

figures.name: Justo Rufino Barrios
figures.blurb: Architect of the 1871 Liberal revolution who modernised Guatemala around coffee exports, curtailed the Church, and expropriated communal lands, entrenching a plantation economy and an authoritarian state.

pullquote.text: By the 1880s coffee accounted for the overwhelming share of Guatemala's exports, and the land and labour to grow it were taken largely from the Maya.

eras.title: Revolution, Coup, and Civil War
eras.standfirst: A decade of democratic reform was cut short by a CIA-backed coup in 1954, opening a thirty-six-year civil war marked by a campaign of genocide against Maya communities.
body: The October Revolution of 1944 ended the Ubico dictatorship and opened what Guatemalans remember as the Ten Years of Spring. The reformist president Juan Jose Arevalo, elected in the country's first free vote, introduced a labour code, social security, and an expansion of schooling. His successor, Colonel Jacobo Arbenz, elected in 1950, pressed further: his 1952 agrarian-reform law, Decree 900, expropriated idle estates, including uncultivated lands of the United Fruit Company, for redistribution to landless peasants.

Arbenz's reforms and his tolerance of communists in public life alarmed Washington and United Fruit. In 1953 the Eisenhower administration authorised the covert Central Intelligence Agency operation known as PBSUCCESS; in June 1954, through an armed exile force under Colonel Carlos Castillo Armas, a propaganda campaign, and the threat of wider intervention, Arbenz was pressured into resignation on 27 June 1954. The coup ended the reform era and installed a military-backed government, beginning decades of repression and the reversal of the agrarian reform.

From 1960 leftist insurgencies took up arms against successive military regimes, opening a civil war that lasted thirty-six years. Counterinsurgency reached its most extreme under General Efrain Rios Montt in 1982-83, when the army razed hundreds of villages in the highlands. The United Nations-backed Commission for Historical Clarification later estimated that some 200,000 people had been killed or forcibly disappeared, the overwhelming majority of them Maya civilians, and concluded that the state had committed acts of genocide against specific Maya groups. The war was ended by the Firm and Lasting Peace agreement signed in Guatemala City on 29 December 1996 between the government and the URNG guerrilla coalition.

events.title: October Revolution
events.summary: A popular uprising ends the Ubico dictatorship and ushers in democratic reform under Juan Jose Arevalo.

events.title: Agrarian reform under Arbenz
events.summary: President Arbenz's Decree 900 expropriates idle lands, including United Fruit estates, for the landless, alarming Washington.

events.title: CIA-backed coup overthrows Arbenz
events.summary: The covert operation PBSUCCESS pressures Arbenz to resign on 27 June 1954, ending the reform era and installing military rule.

events.title: Civil war and the Maya genocide
events.summary: A thirty-six-year conflict leaves some 200,000 dead or disappeared, mostly Maya, with the state held by the CEH to have committed genocide.

events.title: Firm and Lasting Peace accords
events.summary: The government and the URNG sign the final peace agreement in Guatemala City, ending the civil war.

figures.name: Jacobo Arbenz
figures.blurb: Reformist army officer elected in 1950 whose agrarian reform, redistributing idle land including United Fruit holdings, led to his overthrow in the 1954 CIA-backed coup.

figures.name: Efrain Rios Montt
figures.blurb: General who seized power in 1982 and ruled until 1983 during one of the bloodiest phases of the civil war. In 2013 a Guatemalan court convicted him of genocide against the Ixil Maya, though the verdict was later annulled.

pullquote.text: The Commission for Historical Clarification estimated that the armed conflict left more than 200,000 people dead or disappeared, the great majority of them Maya.
pullquote.attribution: On the findings of the UN-backed truth commission

eras.title: Guatemala Since the Peace
eras.standfirst: Since the peace accords Guatemala has been a fragile democracy, struggling with the legacy of war, indigenous rights, corruption, and migration.
body: The 1996 accords ended the fighting but left much of their promise unfulfilled. The truth commission's 1999 report, Guatemala: Memory of Silence, documented the scale of the violence and named the state as principal author of the atrocities; its conclusions framed long campaigns for justice and the eventual, if contested, prosecution of perpetrators. The Maya, who make up a large share of the population, remained politically marginalised and economically disadvantaged, and the pursuit of land, language, and cultural rights stayed central to national life.

Indigenous Guatemala gained a global voice in Rigoberta Menchu, a K'iche' Maya activist whose family was destroyed in the war and who won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1992 for her advocacy of indigenous rights. In the decades after the war, efforts to confront impunity included the United Nations-backed anti-corruption body CICIG, which from 2007 helped secure hundreds of convictions and the resignation of a sitting president in 2015 before it was shut down in 2019. Persistent corruption, weak institutions, violence, and poverty drove large-scale emigration, much of it toward the United States.

In 2023 Guatemala held a remarkable election. The reformist Bernardo Arevalo of the anti-corruption Semilla movement, a son of the 1944 revolution's first president Juan Jose Arevalo, won the presidency against the expectations of the political establishment. Despite repeated legal efforts to overturn the result, he was inaugurated in January 2024, pledging to restore the rule of law. His government's struggle against entrenched corruption defines Guatemala's politics as the republic, heir to one of the world's great ancient civilisations, works to fulfil the unfinished promise of its peace.

events.title: Memory of Silence report
events.summary: The UN-backed Commission for Historical Clarification publishes its findings, attributing the bulk of wartime atrocities to the state and finding acts of genocide against the Maya.

events.title: Rios Montt convicted of genocide
events.summary: A Guatemalan court convicts the former dictator of genocide against the Ixil Maya, the first such verdict by a national court, though it is soon annulled.

events.title: Election of Bernardo Arevalo
events.summary: Anti-corruption candidate Bernardo Arevalo wins the presidency and, despite attempts to block him, is inaugurated in January 2024.

figures.name: Rigoberta Menchu
figures.blurb: K'iche' Maya activist whose family was killed during the civil war and who won the 1992 Nobel Peace Prize for her struggle for the rights of indigenous peoples. She later founded Guatemala's first indigenous political party.

pullquote.text: For her struggle for social justice and ethno-cultural reconciliation based on respect for the rights of indigenous peoples.
pullquote.attribution: Nobel Peace Prize citation for Rigoberta Menchu, 1992

figures.name: Pedro de Alvarado
figures.blurb: Conqueror of Guatemala in 1524 who overthrew the K'iche' and founded the first Spanish capital, inaugurating three centuries of colonial rule.

figures.name: Jacobo Arbenz
figures.blurb: Reformist president whose 1952 agrarian reform precipitated the CIA-backed coup of 1954, ending the country's democratic Ten Years of Spring.

figures.name: Rigoberta Menchu
figures.blurb: K'iche' Maya activist and 1992 Nobel Peace Prize laureate who became the international voice of Guatemala's indigenous peoples during and after the civil war.

sources.label: Foreign Relations of the United States, 1952-1954, Guatemala
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.label: Guatemala Peace Agreements, 1996 (Memory of the World)
Sometimes the Good Guys Win: Guatemala's Shocking 2023 Election
